content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 15, 2006

Ashlee N. Titus
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk, LLP
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-197

Dear Ms. Titus:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the California Dental Association PAC ("CalDPAC") and its chair, Dr. James D. Wood, regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your letter asks several hypothetical questions, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance only, pursuant to Regulation 18329(c). Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c).) The Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTIONS

1. Is CalDPAC a "controlled committee" of Dr. Wood who is the chair of CalDPAC and a member of the City Council for the City of Healdsberg?

2. If the answer to question 1 is yes, would CalDPAC still be a "controlled committee" if any of the following occurs:

A. CalDPAC's bylaws are amended to prohibit the chair from unilaterally authorizing contributions?

B. CalDPAC's bylaws are amended to remove from the chair's authority the power to employ and discharge CalDPAC personnel, legal counsel and accountants?

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000–91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-06-197
Page No. 2


C. CalDPAC's bylaws are amended to prohibit contributions in any local
elections or jurisdictions where a member of its executive committee is an
elected representative or candidate?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. Because Dr. Wood, in his capacity as chair, is a voting member of the
executive committee that decides where political contributions are to be made and
authority to occasionally make unilateral decisions regarding expenditures, he acts jointly
with CalDPAC to make expenditures. Therefore, CalDPAC is a "candidate controlled
committee" under section 82016(a).

2A, B, and C. Yes. Even if the bylaws are amended as you propose in the three
scenarios, Dr. Wood would still be considered to be acting jointly with CalDPAC in
making campaign expenditures because he would remain a voting member of the eleven-
member executive committee which is empowered to make campaign expenditures.

FACTS

The California Dental Association PAC ("CalDPAC"), established over 30 years
ago, is a state general purpose committee sponsored by the California Dental Association
("CDA") that solicits contributions from its membership. CalDPAC makes contributions
primarily to state candidates and has only occasionally contributed to local candidates at
the discretion of its executive committee. Contributions are authorized by a majority vote
of the executive committee. CDA is a California nonprofit, mutual benefit corporation
exempt from income tax pursuant to Internal Revenue Code section 501(c)(6).

CalDPAC is governed by a board of directors currently composed of 56 members,
most of whom are appointed by the local dental societies (the "Board of Directors"). The
chair and treasurer are appointed by the incoming president of CDA.

CalDPAC is managed on a day-to-day basis by an executive committee (the
"Executive Committee") that is also responsible for allocation of funds. The executive
committee is composed of 11 members of the Board of Directors, including the chair and
the treasurer, among others.

The chair is responsible for calling and presiding over the meetings of the Board
of Directors and Executive Committee, and is a voting member. The chair may convene
ad hoc committees to further the purposes of CalDPAC. The chair and the secretary are
responsible for operating the central office of the committee, including employing and
discharging CalDPAC personnel, and employing legal counsel and accountants as
deemed necessary. When time is of the essence, the chair is authorized to make decisions

File No. I-06-197
Page No. 3

regarding contributions by CalDPAC, but must promptly notify the Executive
Committee. The chair is not authorized to sign checks; checks may only be signed by the
treasurer, assistant treasurer, a designated employee of the CDA's finance department,
and a designated employee of the CDA's public policy office.

Dr. Wood became chair of CalDPAC in November 2004. Dr. Wood was a
successful candidate for City Council for the City of Healdsburg, appearing on the
November 7, 2006 ballot.

ANALYSIS

A "controlled committee" is defined in the Act as:

"[A] committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by
a candidate or state measure proponent or that acts jointly
with a candidate, controlled committee, or state measure
proponent in connection with the making of expenditures.
A candidate or state measure proponent controls a
committee if he or she, his or her agent, or any other
committee he or she controls has a significant influence on
the actions or decisions of the committee." (Section
82016.)

Thus, section 82016 describes two ways in which a committee may become a
controlled committee. First, by a candidate exerting significant influence on the actions
or decisions of the committee. Second, by acting jointly with a candidate in connection
with the making of expenditures. (Miller Advice Letter, No. A-91-150; Muller Advice
Letter, No. A-89-305.)

To determine whether a candidate controls a committee under the first part of
section 82016(a), one looks at the degree of the candidate's involvement in the
committee's activities. (Madden Advice Letter, No. A-85-197; Kopp Advice Letter, No.
A-97-108.) The Commission has interpreted the definition of "controlled committee"
broadly to include any significant participation in the actions of a committee by a
candidate, his or her agent, or representatives of any other committee he or she controls.
(Higdon Advice Letter, No. I-94-189; Kopp Advice Letter, supra.) In interpreting section
82016, we have advised that where a candidate is a voting member of an organization's
leadership, the candidate is presumed to be the controlling candidate since the candidate
exercises significant influence on the actions or decisions of the organization. (Ferguson
Advice Letter, No. A-86-044; Gastelum Advice Letter, No. A-96-113.)

As to the second part of section 82016(a), since Dr. Wood is a member of the
executive committee that makes expenditures under the Act, he is considered to be acting

File No. 1-06-197
Page No. 4

jointly with the committee in connection with the making of expenditures under section
82016(a). Therefore, CalDPAC is Dr. Wood's controlled committee.

You ask if the result would change if CalDPAC amends its bylaws (i) to remove
Dr. Wood's authority to unilaterally authorize campaign contributions; (ii) to remove Dr.
Wood's authority to employ and discharge personnel, legal counsel and accountants; or
(iii) to prohibit contributions in any local elections or jurisdiction where an executive
committee member is an elected representative or candidate. Such bylaw amendments
would not divest Dr. Wood of his ability to act jointly with the committee in making
expenditures. (Section 82015.) As a voting member of the eleven-member Executive
Committee that makes contribution decisions for CalDPAC, Dr. Wood would be acting
jointly with the committee. This alone makes it his controlled committee. Accordingly,
adoption of these bylaws changes would not change our conclusion that CalDPAC is a
controlled committee.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:mch
